  • Hide All Code

Module 08: Spatial Social Science

Computational Social Science — Graduate & Undergraduate

2026-04-04

1 Overview

Where things happen is not random. Poverty clusters in specific neighbourhoods. Political parties win in geographically contiguous blocs. Disease spreads outward from an origin. Environmental hazards concentrate near communities least able to fight back. Every social phenomenon has a spatial dimension, and ignoring it can produce systematically wrong conclusions.

This module teaches spatial data analysis from the ground up: loading and describing geographic data, computing spatial autocorrelation, detecting local clusters, and fitting regression models that properly account for spatial dependence.

Learning goals:

  • Load, inspect, and describe spatial data using sf
  • Make informative choropleth maps with ggplot2
  • Understand what a spatial weights matrix is and how to construct one
  • Compute and interpret Moran’s I (global spatial autocorrelation)
  • Read the Moran scatterplot
  • Identify local clusters with LISA statistics
  • Understand when and why spatial regression is needed
  • Fit spatial lag and spatial error models and interpret their output
  • Understand the modifiable areal unit problem (MAUP) — graduate

2 Part A: Working with Spatial Data

2.1 The sf Package

The sf (Simple Features) package is the modern standard for spatial data in R. An sf object is a data frame with a special geometry column that holds geographic information (points, lines, or polygons). Every other column holds attributes about each geographic unit — exactly like a regular data frame, except each row has a location.

library(sf)

# The nc.shp dataset comes bundled with the sf package
# No API key, no download, no authentication required
nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package="sf"), quiet=TRUE)

cat("=== North Carolina County Dataset ===\n")
## === North Carolina County Dataset ===
cat("Class:", class(nc)[1], "\n")
## Class: sf
cat("Rows (counties):", nrow(nc), "\n")
## Rows (counties): 100
cat("Columns:", ncol(nc), "\n")
## Columns: 15
cat("Geometry type:", unique(st_geometry_type(nc)), "\n")
## Geometry type: 7
cat("Coordinate reference system:", st_crs(nc)$input, "\n\n")
## Coordinate reference system: NAD27
# The geometry column — what it looks like
cat("First few rows (data columns only):\n")
## First few rows (data columns only):
nc |> st_drop_geometry() |> select(NAME,AREA,PERIMETER,BIR74,SID74,NWBIR74) |>
  head(6) |> print()
##          NAME  AREA PERIMETER BIR74 SID74 NWBIR74
## 1        Ashe 0.114     1.442  1091     1      10
## 2   Alleghany 0.061     1.231   487     0      10
## 3       Surry 0.143     1.630  3188     5     208
## 4   Currituck 0.070     2.968   508     1     123
## 5 Northampton 0.153     2.206  1421     9    1066
## 6    Hertford 0.097     1.670  1452     7     954

What an sf object is: Think of it as a regular R data frame where every row is a county. The only special thing is the geometry column, which holds the polygon boundaries of each county. You can use dplyr verbs (filter, mutate, select) on an sf object exactly as you would on a regular data frame — the geometry follows along automatically.

# Understand the key variables
cat("Variable descriptions:\n")
## Variable descriptions:
tibble(
  Variable = c("NAME","AREA","PERIMETER","BIR74","SID74","NWBIR74",
               "BIR79","SID79","NWBIR79","geometry"),
  Description = c(
    "County name",
    "County area (square miles)",
    "County perimeter (miles)",
    "Live births 1974–78",
    "Sudden Infant Death Syndrome cases 1974–78",
    "Non-white births 1974–78",
    "Live births 1979–84",
    "SIDS cases 1979–84",
    "Non-white births 1979–84",
    "Polygon boundaries — the geography"
  ),
  Type = c(rep("attribute",9),"geometry")
) |>
  kbl(caption="North Carolina SIDS Dataset Variables") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover","condensed"))
North Carolina SIDS Dataset Variables
Variable Description Type
NAME County name attribute
AREA County area (square miles) attribute
PERIMETER County perimeter (miles) attribute
BIR74 Live births 1974–78 attribute
SID74 Sudden Infant Death Syndrome cases 1974–78 attribute
NWBIR74 Non-white births 1974–78 attribute
BIR79 Live births 1979–84 attribute
SID79 SIDS cases 1979–84 attribute
NWBIR79 Non-white births 1979–84 attribute
geometry Polygon boundaries — the geography geometry 
# Always compute RATES, not raw counts, for mapping
# Raw counts are dominated by population size — more births → more SIDS regardless
nc <- nc |>
  mutate(
    sids_rate74 = SID74 / BIR74 * 1000,  # SIDS per 1,000 live births 1974-78
    sids_rate79 = SID79 / BIR79 * 1000,  # SIDS per 1,000 live births 1979-84
    nw_pct74    = NWBIR74 / BIR74 * 100, # % non-white births 1974-78
    nw_pct79    = NWBIR79 / BIR79 * 100  # % non-white births 1979-84
  )

cat("SIDS rate statistics (per 1,000 births):\n")
## SIDS rate statistics (per 1,000 births):
nc |> st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(sids_rate74, sids_rate79) |>
  summary() |> print()
##   sids_rate74     sids_rate79   
##  Min.   :0.000   Min.   :0.000  
##  1st Qu.:1.084   1st Qu.:1.249  
##  Median :1.855   Median :2.075  
##  Mean   :2.046   Mean   :2.039  
##  3rd Qu.:2.604   3rd Qu.:2.539  
##  Max.   :9.554   Max.   :6.114

Critical distinction — counts vs. rates: A map of raw SIDS deaths will show highest values in the most populous counties. This is just population distribution, not SIDS risk. Always map rates when comparing areas of different population size. The rate (deaths per 1,000 births) controls for how many births there were, making counties comparable.

2.2 Your First Map

# Project to NAD83 / NC State Plane for better visual proportions
nc_proj <- st_transform(nc, 32119)

ggplot(nc_proj) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill=sids_rate74), colour="white", linewidth=0.3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option    = "plasma",
    name      = "SIDS per\n1,000 births",
    direction = -1,
    na.value  = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "SIDS Rates in North Carolina Counties, 1974–1978",
    subtitle = paste("Darker = higher rate. Look for geographic clustering:",
                     "are high-rate counties near each other?"),
    caption  = "Source: nc.shp (sf package). Cressie (1991)."
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold"),
        plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40"))

Reading a choropleth map: Scan for areas where similar colours cluster together — this visual pattern is spatial autocorrelation. If the map looks like a random patchwork (salt-and-pepper), there is little spatial structure. If you see contiguous regions of similar shade, there is clustering. The question is whether what you see is statistically significant or could be chance.

2.3 Describing the Distribution

cat("=== DESCRIPTIVE STATISTICS ===\n\n")
## === DESCRIPTIVE STATISTICS ===
cat("SIDS rate 1974-78:\n")
## SIDS rate 1974-78:
cat("  Mean:", round(mean(nc$sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE), 3), "per 1,000 births\n")
##   Mean: 2.046 per 1,000 births
cat("  SD:  ", round(sd(nc$sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE), 3), "\n")
##   SD:   1.573
cat("  Min: ", round(min(nc$sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE), 3),
    "  Max:", round(max(nc$sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE), 3), "\n")
##   Min:  0   Max: 9.554
cat("  Counties with rate = 0:", sum(nc$sids_rate74 == 0, na.rm=TRUE), "\n\n")
##   Counties with rate = 0: 13
# Distribution plot
nc |> st_drop_geometry() |>
  ggplot(aes(x=sids_rate74)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth=0.5, fill="#2C6FAC", colour="white", alpha=0.85) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept=mean(sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE)),
             colour="#C0392B", linetype="dashed", linewidth=1) +
  annotate("text",
           x=mean(nc$sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE)+0.3,
           y=15,
           label=paste0("Mean = ",
             round(mean(nc$sids_rate74, na.rm=TRUE),2)),
           hjust=0, colour="#C0392B") +
  labs(title="Distribution of SIDS Rates Across NC Counties (1974-78)",
       x="SIDS per 1,000 live births", y="Number of counties",
       subtitle="Note: right-skewed distribution. A few high-rate counties pull the mean above the median.")

2.4 Bivariate Relationship Before Spatial Analysis

Before any spatial modelling, understand the basic bivariate relationship:

# Does % non-white births predict SIDS rates?
cor_val <- cor(nc$sids_rate74, nc$nw_pct74, use="complete.obs")

ggplot(nc |> st_drop_geometry(),
       aes(x=nw_pct74, y=sids_rate74)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.7, colour="#2C6FAC", size=2.5) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", colour="#C0392B", se=TRUE, linewidth=1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method="loess", colour="#27AE60", se=FALSE,
              linetype="dashed", linewidth=1) +
  annotate("text", x=70, y=0.2, hjust=1, size=3.5,
           label=paste0("r = ", round(cor_val, 3))) +
  labs(
    title    = "% Non-White Births vs. SIDS Rate — NC Counties 1974-78",
    subtitle = "Red = OLS fit; Green dashed = LOESS (non-parametric). If lines diverge, relationship is nonlinear.",
    x="% Non-white births",
    y="SIDS per 1,000 births",
    caption  = "Each point = one of 100 NC counties."
  )

cat("Pearson correlation: r =", round(cor_val, 3), "\n")
## Pearson correlation: r = 0.581
cat("Interpretation: ", abs(round(cor_val,3)),
    ifelse(abs(cor_val) > 0.5, "(moderate to strong)", "(weak to moderate)"),
    "positive association.\n")
## Interpretation:  0.581 (moderate to strong) positive association.
cat("Higher % non-white births → higher SIDS rate.\n")
## Higher % non-white births → higher SIDS rate.
cat("BUT: this could be confounded by poverty, healthcare access, etc.\n")
## BUT: this could be confounded by poverty, healthcare access, etc.

3 Part B: Measuring Spatial Autocorrelation

3.1 Spatial Weights Matrix

Before computing Moran’s I, you need to define what “nearby” means — the spatial weights matrix W. This is a substantive modelling choice, not just a technical step.

library(spdep)

nc_sp   <- as(nc_proj, "Spatial")
nb_queen <- poly2nb(nc_sp, queen=TRUE)  # Queen contiguity: share any boundary point

cat("=== SPATIAL WEIGHTS (Queen Contiguity) ===\n\n")
## === SPATIAL WEIGHTS (Queen Contiguity) ===
cat("Definition: Two counties are neighbours if they share any border point.\n\n")
## Definition: Two counties are neighbours if they share any border point.
cat("Summary:\n")
## Summary:
cat("  Counties:", length(nb_queen), "\n")
##   Counties: 100
cat("  Average neighbours:", round(mean(card(nb_queen)), 2), "\n")
##   Average neighbours: 4.9
cat("  Min neighbours:", min(card(nb_queen)),
    "(island county — few borders)\n")
##   Min neighbours: 2 (island county — few borders)
cat("  Max neighbours:", max(card(nb_queen)), "\n\n")
##   Max neighbours: 9
# Row-standardise: each county's neighbours' weights sum to 1
# Makes spatial lag = weighted average of neighbours' values
lw <- nb2listw(nb_queen, style="W", zero.policy=TRUE)

cat("Row-standardised weights applied.\n")
## Row-standardised weights applied.
cat("Result: spatial lag of Y[i] = average Y across i's neighbours.\n")
## Result: spatial lag of Y[i] = average Y across i's neighbours.

Neighbourhood definitions compared:

Type Definition When to Use
Queen contiguity Share any boundary point (edge or corner) Default for county/census data
Rook contiguity Share a boundary edge only (no corners) When corners are unlikely to create real spillovers
k-nearest neighbours K geographically closest units When units vary greatly in size; ensures all have same number of neighbours
Distance-based Units within d km are neighbours When a specific spatial range is theoretically meaningful
Inverse distance Weight decreases with distance When proximity matters continuously, not just inside/outside a threshold

3.2 Global Moran’s I

Moran’s I answers the question: overall, do similar values cluster together geographically?

\[I = \frac{N}{\sum_i \sum_j w_{ij}} \cdot \frac{\sum_i \sum_j w_{ij}(y_i - \bar{y})(y_j - \bar{y})}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2}\]

In plain English: it measures whether a county’s deviation from the mean tends to match its neighbours’ deviations. Positive I = similar values cluster; negative I = dissimilar values cluster (chess-board pattern); I ≈ 0 = random spatial distribution.

moran_result <- moran.mc(
  nc_proj$sids_rate74,
  listw       = lw,
  nsim        = 999,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

cat("=== GLOBAL MORAN'S I — SIDS RATES 1974-78 ===\n\n")
## === GLOBAL MORAN'S I — SIDS RATES 1974-78 ===
cat("Observed Moran's I:", round(moran_result$statistic, 4), "\n")
## Observed Moran's I: 0.2309
cat("Expected I (random):  ", round(mean(moran_result$res), 4), "\n")
## Expected I (random):   -0.0127
cat("p-value (permutation):", round(moran_result$p.value, 4), "\n")
## p-value (permutation): 0.001
cat("N permutations:        999\n\n")
## N permutations:        999
cat("INTERPRETATION:\n")
## INTERPRETATION:
if (moran_result$p.value < 0.05 && moran_result$statistic > 0) {
  cat("→ Significant positive spatial autocorrelation (p <",
      round(moran_result$p.value, 3), ").\n")
  cat("→ High-rate counties cluster together; low-rate counties cluster together.\n")
  cat("→ The geographic pattern is NOT random — something drives this clustering.\n")
} else {
  cat("→ No significant spatial autocorrelation detected.\n")
  cat("→ SIDS rates appear randomly distributed across counties.\n")
}
## → Significant positive spatial autocorrelation (p < 0.001 ).
## → High-rate counties cluster together; low-rate counties cluster together.
## → The geographic pattern is NOT random — something drives this clustering.
moran.plot(
  x           = nc_proj$sids_rate74,
  listw       = lw,
  xlab        = "SIDS rate (standardised)",
  ylab        = "Spatially lagged SIDS rate (neighbours' average, standardised)",
  main        = "Moran Scatterplot: SIDS Rates 1974-78",
  zero.policy = TRUE
)
abline(h=0, v=0, lty=2, col="grey60")

Reading the Moran scatterplot step by step:

  1. X-axis: Each county’s own SIDS rate, standardised (mean=0, SD=1)
  2. Y-axis: The average SIDS rate of that county’s neighbours, standardised
  3. The slope of the regression line drawn through the points IS Moran’s I
  4. Four quadrants:
    • Upper-right (HH): County and neighbours both above average — high cluster
    • Lower-left (LL): County and neighbours both below average — low cluster
    • Upper-left (LH): County below average but neighbours above — spatial outlier
    • Lower-right (HL): County above average but neighbours below — spatial outlier
  5. Influential points (circled by plot() in the outer zones) are counties that depart strongly from the spatial trend — worth examining individually

4 Part C: Local Clusters (LISA)

4.1 What LISA Tells You That Global Moran’s I Cannot

Global Moran’s I tells you whether clustering exists. LISA (Local Indicators of Spatial Association) tells you where it exists and what type.

lmoran <- localmoran(
  nc_proj$sids_rate74,
  listw       = lw,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

nc_lisa <- nc_proj |>
  mutate(
    local_I  = lmoran[,"Ii"],
    local_p  = p.adjust(lmoran[,"Pr(z != E(Ii))"], method = "BH"),  # BH correction
    z_rate   = as.numeric(scale(sids_rate74)),
    z_lag    = as.numeric(scale(lag.listw(lw, sids_rate74, zero.policy=TRUE))),
    lisa_type = case_when(
      local_p > 0.05                   ~ "Not significant",
      z_rate > 0 & z_lag > 0           ~ "High-High (hot spot)",
      z_rate < 0 & z_lag < 0           ~ "Low-Low  (cold spot)",
      z_rate > 0 & z_lag < 0           ~ "High-Low  (outlier)",
      z_rate < 0 & z_lag > 0           ~ "Low-High  (outlier)",
      TRUE                             ~ "Not significant"
    ),
    lisa_type = factor(lisa_type,
      levels=c("High-High (hot spot)","Low-Low  (cold spot)",
               "High-Low  (outlier)","Low-High  (outlier)","Not significant"))
  )

cat("=== LISA CLUSTER TYPE DISTRIBUTION ===\n")
## === LISA CLUSTER TYPE DISTRIBUTION ===
print(table(nc_lisa$lisa_type))
## 
## High-High (hot spot) Low-Low  (cold spot)  High-Low  (outlier) 
##                    2                    0                    0 
##  Low-High  (outlier)      Not significant 
##                    1                   97
ggplot(nc_lisa) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill=lisa_type), colour="white", linewidth=0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "High-High (hot spot)"  = "#C0392B",
      "Low-Low  (cold spot)"  = "#2C6FAC",
      "High-Low  (outlier)"   = "#E67E22",
      "Low-High  (outlier)"   = "#8E44AD",
      "Not significant"       = "grey88"
    ),
    name="LISA Type", drop=FALSE
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "LISA Cluster Map: SIDS Rates, NC 1974–78",
    subtitle = paste(
      "Red = high-rate counties surrounded by high-rate neighbours (hot spots).",
      "\nBlue = low-rate surrounded by low-rate (cold spots). Grey = not significant.",
      "\nOrange/purple = spatial outliers (county differs from its neighbours)."
    ),
    caption  = "Significance: p < .05 after Benjamini-Hochberg correction."
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold"), legend.position="bottom")

Reading the LISA map: Each coloured county is part of a statistically significant spatial cluster. Red (High-High) counties are a public health concern — they form geographic concentrations of elevated SIDS risk. Blue (Low-Low) counties are in a geographic region of low risk. Purple and orange counties are anomalies — they do not fit the surrounding pattern and deserve investigation: are they data errors? Do they have unusual characteristics that set them apart from their neighbours?

5 Part D: Spatial Regression

5.1 Why OLS Fails with Spatial Data

If your outcome variable is spatially autocorrelated, the residuals from an OLS regression are almost certainly spatially autocorrelated too. This violates OLS’s independence assumption and has two consequences:

  1. Biased coefficients — if the omitted spatially-clustered variable is a confounder
  2. Underestimated standard errors — false precision; you reject H₀ too often

The Lagrange Multiplier diagnostic tests (from an OLS model) tell you whether the problem is in the outcome (spatial lag) or in the errors (spatial error):

# First, fit a standard OLS baseline
ols_m <- lm(sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74),
            data = nc_proj |> st_drop_geometry())

cat("=== OLS BASELINE MODEL ===\n")
## === OLS BASELINE MODEL ===
summary(ols_m)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data = st_drop_geometry(nc_proj))
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.0065 -0.7824 -0.2284  0.5419  6.2830 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -0.802545   1.019250  -0.787    0.433    
## nw_pct74     0.043470   0.006169   7.047 2.67e-10 ***
## log(BIR74)   0.195384   0.131058   1.491    0.139    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.28 on 97 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3519, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3385 
## F-statistic: 26.33 on 2 and 97 DF,  p-value: 7.343e-10
lm_tests <- lm.LMtests(ols_m, listw=lw,
                         test=c("LMlag","LMerr","RLMlag","RLMerr"),
                         zero.policy=TRUE)

cat("\n=== LAGRANGE MULTIPLIER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS ===\n")
## 
## === LAGRANGE MULTIPLIER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS ===
cat("(Do OLS residuals show spatial autocorrelation?)\n\n")
## (Do OLS residuals show spatial autocorrelation?)
print(lm_tests)
## 
##  Rao's score (a.k.a Lagrange multiplier) diagnostics for spatial
##  dependence
## 
## data:  
## model: lm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data =
## st_drop_geometry(nc_proj))
## test weights: listw
## 
## RSlag = 0.018854, df = 1, p-value = 0.8908
## 
## 
##  Rao's score (a.k.a Lagrange multiplier) diagnostics for spatial
##  dependence
## 
## data:  
## model: lm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data =
## st_drop_geometry(nc_proj))
## test weights: listw
## 
## RSerr = 0.63229, df = 1, p-value = 0.4265
## 
## 
##  Rao's score (a.k.a Lagrange multiplier) diagnostics for spatial
##  dependence
## 
## data:  
## model: lm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data =
## st_drop_geometry(nc_proj))
## test weights: listw
## 
## adjRSlag = 1.8965, df = 1, p-value = 0.1685
## 
## 
##  Rao's score (a.k.a Lagrange multiplier) diagnostics for spatial
##  dependence
## 
## data:  
## model: lm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data =
## st_drop_geometry(nc_proj))
## test weights: listw
## 
## adjRSerr = 2.51, df = 1, p-value = 0.1131
cat("\nDECISION RULE:\n")
## 
## DECISION RULE:
cat("  LMlag significant, LMerr not → fit Spatial Lag Model\n")
##   LMlag significant, LMerr not → fit Spatial Lag Model
cat("  LMerr significant, LMlag not → fit Spatial Error Model\n")
##   LMerr significant, LMlag not → fit Spatial Error Model
cat("  Both significant → compare RLMlag vs RLMerr; choose larger one\n")
##   Both significant → compare RLMlag vs RLMerr; choose larger one
cat("  Neither significant → OLS residuals are clean; no spatial model needed\n")
##   Neither significant → OLS residuals are clean; no spatial model needed

5.2 The Spatial Lag Model

When to use it: When you believe the outcome in one county is directly influenced by the outcome in neighbouring counties. Sociological example: unemployment in one county pulls down economic activity in adjacent counties. Epidemiological example: SIDS rates may spread through shared environmental exposures that cross county lines.

The model: \[y = \rho Wy + X\beta + \varepsilon\]

  • \(Wy\) = spatially lagged outcome (weighted average of neighbours’ Y)
  • \(\rho\) = spatial autoregressive parameter
  • If \(\rho > 0\): a county’s outcome is pulled toward its neighbours’ outcomes
  • If \(\rho = 0\): no spatial spillover; OLS is appropriate
library(spatialreg)

slm <- lagsarlm(sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74),
                data      = nc_proj |> st_drop_geometry(),
                listw     = lw,
                zero.policy = TRUE)

cat("=== SPATIAL LAG MODEL ===\n")
## === SPATIAL LAG MODEL ===
summary(slm)
## 
## Call:
## lagsarlm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data = st_drop_geometry(nc_proj), 
##     listw = lw, zero.policy = TRUE)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.99565 -0.79017 -0.22967  0.55187  6.30313 
## 
## Type: lag 
## Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
##               Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)
## (Intercept) -0.8016249  1.0038828 -0.7985    0.4246
## nw_pct74     0.0429463  0.0074382  5.7738 7.752e-09
## log(BIR74)   0.1922893  0.1301325  1.4776    0.1395
## 
## Rho: 0.019187, LR test value: 0.019449, p-value: 0.88909
## Asymptotic standard error: 0.13487
##     z-value: 0.14226, p-value: 0.88687
## Wald statistic: 0.020239, p-value: 0.88687
## 
## Log likelihood: -165.0191 for lag model
## ML residual variance (sigma squared): 1.5879, (sigma: 1.2601)
## Number of observations: 100 
## Number of parameters estimated: 5 
## AIC: 340.04, (AIC for lm: 338.06)
## LM test for residual autocorrelation
## test value: 2.4244, p-value: 0.11946

Reading the spatial lag output:

Reading Spatial Lag Model Output
Section What It Means
rho (ρ) The spatial autoregressive parameter. Positive = outcomes pull toward neighbours’ outcomes. Negative = repulsion (unusual in social data). Significant ρ = spatial spillovers are real.
Asymptotic standard error of rho Standard error of ρ. Used to compute the z-test below.
z-value for rho ρ / SE(ρ). Test of H₀: ρ = 0 (no spatial lag needed).
LR test of spatial autoregressive coefficient Likelihood ratio test comparing the spatial lag model to OLS. Significant = spatial lag model fits significantly better.
Coefficients: nw_pct74 Effect of % non-white on SIDS rate, after accounting for spatial dependence. Compare to OLS coefficient — if different, the spatial structure was confounding the OLS estimate.
Coefficients: log(BIR74) Controlling for birth volume (larger counties have more stable rates).
AIC / BIC Model fit. Compare to OLS AIC: lower = better. If spatial model’s AIC < OLS AIC, spatial model is preferred.
Log likelihood Higher (less negative) = better fit. Always improves over OLS when ρ is nonzero.

5.3 The Spatial Error Model

When to use it: When spatial autocorrelation arises from omitted spatially-clustered variables — not from direct outcome spillovers, but from a common omitted cause.

The model: \[y = X\beta + u, \quad u = \lambda Wu + \varepsilon\]

  • \(\lambda\) = spatial error autocorrelation
  • A significant \(\lambda\) means the errors cluster spatially — something unmeasured that is geographically concentrated is driving the outcome
sem <- errorsarlm(sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74),
                  data        = nc_proj |> st_drop_geometry(),
                  listw       = lw,
                  zero.policy = TRUE)

cat("=== SPATIAL ERROR MODEL ===\n")
## === SPATIAL ERROR MODEL ===
summary(sem)
## 
## Call:
## errorsarlm(formula = sids_rate74 ~ nw_pct74 + log(BIR74), data = st_drop_geometry(nc_proj), 
##     listw = lw, zero.policy = TRUE)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.99135 -0.77582 -0.22271  0.56245  6.27218 
## 
## Type: error 
## Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
##               Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)
## (Intercept) -0.7475285  1.0419483 -0.7174    0.4731
## nw_pct74     0.0445219  0.0065724  6.7740 1.252e-11
## log(BIR74)   0.1845284  0.1334634  1.3826    0.1668
## 
## Lambda: 0.11255, LR test value: 0.60413, p-value: 0.43701
## Asymptotic standard error: 0.14353
##     z-value: 0.78417, p-value: 0.43294
## Wald statistic: 0.61492, p-value: 0.43294
## 
## Log likelihood: -164.7267 for error model
## ML residual variance (sigma squared): 1.5745, (sigma: 1.2548)
## Number of observations: 100 
## Number of parameters estimated: 5 
## AIC: 339.45, (AIC for lm: 338.06)

5.4 Model Comparison

# Compare all three models
cat("=== MODEL COMPARISON ===\n\n")
## === MODEL COMPARISON ===
comparison <- tibble(
  Model    = c("OLS","Spatial Lag","Spatial Error"),
  AIC      = round(c(AIC(ols_m), AIC(slm), AIC(sem)), 3),
  LogLik   = round(c(logLik(ols_m), logLik(slm), logLik(sem)), 3),
  nw_coef  = round(c(coef(ols_m)["nw_pct74"],
                     coef(slm)["nw_pct74"],
                     coef(sem)["nw_pct74"]), 4),
  spatial_param = c(NA_real_,
                    round(slm$rho, 4),
                    round(sem$lambda, 4))
)

kbl(comparison,
    caption="OLS vs. Spatial Models Comparison",
    col.names=c("Model","AIC","Log-Lik","nw_pct74 coef","ρ or λ")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  row_spec(which.min(comparison$AIC), bold=TRUE, background="#EAFAF1") |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Bold/green row = best-fitting model (lowest AIC).",
    "\nIf the OLS nw_pct74 coefficient changes in the spatial models,",
    "spatial structure was confounding the OLS estimate."
  ))
OLS vs. Spatial Models Comparison
Model AIC Log-Lik nw_pct74 coef ρ or λ
OLS 338.058 -165.029 0.0435 NA
Spatial Lag 340.038 -165.019 0.0429 0.0192
Spatial Error 339.453 -164.727 0.0445 0.1125
Note:
Bold/green row = best-fitting model (lowest AIC).
If the OLS nw_pct74 coefficient changes in the spatial models, spatial structure was confounding the OLS estimate. 
# Critical check: have the spatial models removed residual autocorrelation?
cat("=== RESIDUAL SPATIAL AUTOCORRELATION ===\n")
## === RESIDUAL SPATIAL AUTOCORRELATION ===
cat("(Did the spatial model actually fix the problem?)\n\n")
## (Did the spatial model actually fix the problem?)
tests <- list(
  OLS = moran.mc(residuals(ols_m), lw, 999, zero.policy=TRUE),
  SLM = moran.mc(residuals(slm),   lw, 999, zero.policy=TRUE),
  SEM = moran.mc(residuals(sem),   lw, 999, zero.policy=TRUE)
)

tibble(
  Model   = names(tests),
  Moran_I = sapply(tests, \(t) round(t$statistic, 4)),
  p_value = sapply(tests, \(t) round(t$p.value, 4)),
  Clean   = ifelse(sapply(tests, \(t) t$p.value) > 0.05,
                   "✓ Residuals clean", "✗ Still autocorrelated")
) |>
  kbl(caption="Moran's I in Residuals After Each Model") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  footnote(general="Goal: p > .05 for residuals = model has removed the spatial structure.")
Moran’s I in Residuals After Each Model
Model Moran_I p_value Clean
OLS 0.0531 0.148 ✓ Residuals clean
SLM 0.0439 0.190 ✓ Residuals clean
SEM -0.0039 0.424 ✓ Residuals clean
Note:
Goal: p > .05 for residuals = model has removed the spatial structure.

6 Part E: 🟥 The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP)

Any analysis using administrative boundaries (counties, census tracts, ZIP codes) is subject to the MAUP: the results depend partly on how the boundaries are drawn. Different boundary systems for the same underlying data can produce different, even contradictory, results.

cat("=== MAUP DEMONSTRATION ===\n\n")
## === MAUP DEMONSTRATION ===
# Show that results change at different geographic scales
# We cannot directly aggregate NC counties into regions without more data,
# but we can demonstrate by correlating at different granularities

# County-level correlation (100 units)
r_county <- cor(nc$sids_rate74, nc$nw_pct74, use="complete.obs")

# "Region-level" (aggregate 5 geographic regions by CNTY_ range)
nc_region <- nc |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  mutate(region = cut(CNTY_, breaks=5, labels=paste("Region",1:5))) |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(
    mean_sids = weighted.mean(sids_rate74, BIR74, na.rm=TRUE),
    mean_nw   = weighted.mean(nw_pct74, BIR74, na.rm=TRUE),
    .groups   = "drop"
  )
r_region <- cor(nc_region$mean_sids, nc_region$mean_nw)

cat("Correlation between SIDS rate and % non-white births:\n\n")
## Correlation between SIDS rate and % non-white births:
cat("  At county level  (N=100 units): r =", round(r_county, 3), "\n")
##   At county level  (N=100 units): r = 0.581
cat("  At region level  (N=5 units):   r =", round(r_region, 3), "\n\n")
##   At region level  (N=5 units):   r = 0.737
cat("Same data, same variables, different geographic aggregation → different result.\n")
## Same data, same variables, different geographic aggregation → different result.
cat("This is the MAUP. Neither result is 'wrong' — they answer different questions.\n\n")
## This is the MAUP. Neither result is 'wrong' — they answer different questions.
cat("PRACTICAL IMPLICATION: The geographic scale of your analysis should match\n")
## PRACTICAL IMPLICATION: The geographic scale of your analysis should match
cat("the geographic scale at which the process you study actually operates.\n")
## the geographic scale at which the process you study actually operates.
cat("Neighbourhood effects operate at the tract level, not the county level.\n")
## Neighbourhood effects operate at the tract level, not the county level.
cat("Labour markets operate at the metropolitan area level, not the county level.\n")
## Labour markets operate at the metropolitan area level, not the county level.

7 A Complete Spatial Analysis Checklist

Spatial Analysis Workflow — Step-by-Step Checklist
Step Action Tool
1 Load spatial data; verify CRS, N units, and variable types st_read(), st_crs(), nrow()
2 Compute rates (not raw counts) if comparing areas of different size mutate(rate = count/pop * 1000)
3 Map the outcome variable and visually assess clustering ggplot() + geom_sf(aes(fill=rate))
4 Describe the univariate distribution (histogram, summary statistics) summary(), hist(), ggplot(aes(x=rate))
5 Examine the bivariate relationship between key predictor and outcome ggplot(aes(x=predictor, y=rate)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth()
6 Define and justify the spatial weights matrix poly2nb() + nb2listw()
7 Compute global Moran’s I; draw Moran scatterplot moran.mc() + moran.plot()
8 If Moran’s I significant: run LISA to find local clusters localmoran() + LISA map
9 Fit OLS baseline; run LM diagnostic tests lm() + lm.LMtests()
10 Fit appropriate spatial model; verify residuals are clean (Moran’s I not significant) lagsarlm() or errorsarlm() + moran.mc(residuals())

8 Common Mistakes

Mistake Consequence Fix
Mapping raw counts High-population areas dominate — meaningless Always map rates (per 1,000 or per 100,000)
Ignoring the weights matrix choice Different W → different Moran’s I Report your W choice; do sensitivity analysis
Not correcting LISA for multiple testing ~5 false positives per 100 tests at p=.05 Always use p.adjust.method="BH" in localmoran()
Fitting spatial model without checking OLS first Can’t justify model choice without diagnostics Always run LM tests from OLS before choosing spatial model
Not checking residuals after spatial model Model might not have fixed the problem Always run Moran’s I on residuals of the spatial model
Treating MAUP as a technicality Conclusions may reverse at different scales Match geographic scale to theoretical process; do sensitivity analysis

9 Check Your Understanding

1. Explain in plain language why a raw count of SIDS deaths would be a misleading variable to map across counties. What would the map show, and what would you mistakenly conclude?

2. You compute Moran’s I = 0.38 (p = .002) for county-level poverty rates. Interpret this result precisely: what does it say about the geographic distribution of poverty?

3. A LISA analysis identifies three High-High counties and two Low-Low counties as statistically significant. A policy analyst wants to target intervention resources at the High-High counties. What additional information would you want before accepting this recommendation?

4. Your LM diagnostic tests show LMlag p = .031 and LMerr p = .003. The robust tests show RLMlag p = .210 and RLMerr p = .012. Which model should you fit? Explain your reasoning.

5. 🟥 You are studying the relationship between school funding and test scores across school districts. You fit OLS and find a positive coefficient for funding. Residual Moran’s I = 0.28 (p < .001). You fit a spatial error model and the funding coefficient drops from 0.15 to 0.09. Explain in substantive terms what this change means. What unmeasured, spatially concentrated variable might explain it?
